title: Borderline Personality Disorder with Non-Suicidal Self-Injury on an Inpatient Psychiatric Unit in a 24-Year-Old: Managing SPLITTING and Limit-Testing with a CONSISTENT, Unified Team Care Plan, Clear and Compassionate Limit-Setting, Ongoing Self-Harm/Suicide-Risk Assessment, Therapeutic Communication, Staff Self-Awareness (Countertransference), and Referral to Dialectical Behavior Therapy
subtitle: BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER (BPD) = a pervasive pattern of INSTABILITY in interpersonal relationships, self-image, and affect, with marked IMPULSIVITY, beginning by early adulthood. Core features: frantic efforts to avoid real or imagined ABANDONMENT; a pattern of intense, UNSTABLE relationships alternating between idealization and devaluation; IDENTITY disturbance (unstable self-image); IMPULSIVITY in ≥2 potentially self-damaging areas (spending, sex, substance use, reckless driving, binge eating); recurrent SUICIDAL behavior/gestures/threats or SELF-MUTILATING behavior; AFFECTIVE INSTABILITY/marked mood reactivity; chronic feelings of EMPTINESS; inappropriate, intense ANGER or difficulty controlling anger; and transient, stress-related PARANOID ideation or DISSOCIATION. NON-SUICIDAL SELF-INJURY (NSSI) — e.g., cutting/burning — is common in BPD and often serves to RELIEVE intense emotional pain, end dissociation/numbness, or self-punish; it is DISTINCT from suicidal intent (the goal is not death), BUT BPD also carries a substantially elevated risk of actual SUICIDE, so the nurse must assess self-harm and SUICIDE RISK SEPARATELY and repeatedly (never assume an act is 'just attention-seeking'). SPLITTING is the hallmark defense mechanism: the patient unconsciously views people/situations as ALL-GOOD or ALL-BAD and cannot integrate the two, often IDEALIZING some staff ('you're the only one who truly understands me') while DEVALUING others ('that other nurse is cruel and incompetent') — which can DIVIDE the team and lead staff to disagree about the patient or be manipulated into bending rules. The MOST IMPORTANT nursing response to splitting is a CONSISTENT, UNIFIED TEAM APPROACH: the entire team follows the SAME clearly-written CARE PLAN with the SAME limits, COMMUNICATES regularly (team huddles, handoffs, documentation) to share information and stay aligned, does NOT take sides or accept the role of the 'special'/idealized staff member, and does not allow the patient to play staff against one another. LIMIT-SETTING is central and therapeutic (not punitive): establish CLEAR, CONSISTENT, REASONABLE limits and boundaries; state them MATTER-OF-FACTLY and apply them uniformly across all staff and shifts; follow through with consistent, non-punitive consequences; AVOID power struggles, arguments, and personalizing the patient's behavior; maintain professional BOUNDARIES (no favoritism, no self-disclosure that fosters idealization, no 'special' exceptions); and present limits as caring structure, not rejection. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION: be empathetic AND boundaried; VALIDATE the patient's emotions ('it makes sense you feel overwhelmed') WITHOUT endorsing maladaptive behavior; be honest, reliable, and consistent (which itself counters the fear of abandonment); avoid rejecting, shaming, punitive, or sarcastic responses; help the patient label feelings and use coping/distress-tolerance skills instead of self-harm. STAFF SELF-AWARENESS / COUNTERTRANSFERENCE: patients with BPD predictably evoke strong reactions in staff — anger/frustration, feeling manipulated, or 'rescue' fantasies/over-involvement in idealized staff — and staff may unconsciously act these out (being punitive, or being the 'special' rule-bender); recognizing and PROCESSING these reactions (supervision, team support, debriefing) is essential to maintaining a consistent therapeutic stance. SELF-HARM/SAFETY MANAGEMENT: maintain a SAFE milieu (remove/limit means of self-harm, appropriate observation level), assess suicide and self-harm risk on an ongoing basis, respond to self-injury MATTER-OF-FACTLY (provide necessary wound care without excessive attention/drama that could reinforce the behavior, while never being dismissive of risk), help the patient identify triggers/feelings and substitute coping skills, and involve the patient in safety planning. TREATMENT: DIALECTICAL BEHAVIOR THERAPY (DBT) is the gold-standard, evidence-based psychotherapy for BPD (skills in MINDFULNESS, DISTRESS TOLERANCE, EMOTION REGULATION, and INTERPERSONAL EFFECTIVENESS); other psychotherapies (mentalization-based therapy, schema therapy, transference-focused therapy) also help; PSYCHOTHERAPY is the mainstay, while MEDICATIONS are adjunctive and target specific symptoms (mood instability, impulsivity, transient psychosis) — there is no medication that 'cures' BPD, and clinicians AVOID benzodiazepines (disinhibition, dependence, overdose risk) and excessive polypharmacy; treat COMORBIDITIES (depression, PTSD, substance use, eating disorders, which are common). PROGNOSIS: with treatment (especially DBT) and over time, many patients improve substantially and achieve remission. The MANAGEMENT-OF-CARE/CARE-COORDINATION competencies are paramount here: building and maintaining a UNIFIED interdisciplinary CARE PLAN, COORDINATING and aligning the whole team (nursing across shifts, providers, social work, therapists) to deliver consistent limits and a consistent therapeutic stance, preventing staff splitting through communication, and arranging continuity (DBT/outpatient referral, follow-up). NURSING ROLE: deliver consistent, compassionate, boundaried care within a unified team plan; set and maintain clear limits; assess and manage self-harm/suicide risk; communicate therapeutically while validating emotions without reinforcing maladaptive behavior; manage personal/team countertransference; and coordinate evidence-based treatment (DBT) and follow-up

patient.name: A. Rivera
patient.code: Full code; alert with capacity; admitted to an inpatient psychiatric unit; goal = safety, stabilization, a consistent therapeutic milieu, skills-building, and linkage to evidence-based treatment (DBT)
patient.admitDate: Admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit after a NON-SUICIDAL SELF-INJURY episode (superficial forearm cutting) precipitated by a relationship breakup, with intense emotional distress and fear of abandonment. She has a known diagnosis of BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER. On the unit she is already exhibiting SPLITTING — telling her assigned nurse 'you're the only one here who actually cares; the night nurse is cruel and doesn't understand me' — along with limit-testing (requests for special privileges/exceptions), affective instability, and intense anger when limits are held. The team must provide a safe milieu, a consistent unified approach, clear compassionate limits, ongoing risk assessment, and treatment linkage.
patient.room: Inpatient psychiatric unit (safe milieu, appropriate observation level); interdisciplinary team (psychiatry, psychiatric nursing across shifts, social work, DBT/therapy); structured environment

labs.n: PRESENTATION + BPD features + NSSI vs suicide risk
labs.v: 24 y/o woman A. Rivera, admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit after a NON-SUICIDAL SELF-INJURY (NSSI) episode — superficial forearm cutting — precipitated by a relationship breakup, with intense emotional distress and fear of abandonment. She carries a known diagnosis of BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER (BPD): a pervasive pattern of INSTABILITY in relationships, self-image, and affect with marked IMPULSIVITY, including frantic efforts to avoid ABANDONMENT, intense unstable relationships that swing between IDEALIZATION and DEVALUATION, identity disturbance, impulsivity, recurrent self-harm/suicidal gestures, affective instability/mood reactivity, chronic emptiness, intense inappropriate anger, and transient stress-related paranoia/dissociation. NSSI in BPD (cutting, burning) typically functions to RELIEVE overwhelming emotional pain, to end dissociation/numbness, or to self-punish — its goal is NOT death, so it is DISTINCT from a suicide attempt; HOWEVER, BPD carries a substantially ELEVATED risk of actual SUICIDE, so the nurse assesses SELF-HARM and SUICIDE RISK SEPARATELY and on an ONGOING basis, and never dismisses self-injury as merely 'attention-seeking' (a stigmatizing, dangerous misjudgment). On admission her superficial lacerations were cleaned and dressed matter-of-factly; she is tearful and emotionally dysregulated, voicing fear of abandonment, and states the cutting 'made the pain stop for a minute.' She denies current suicidal intent/plan today, but ongoing risk assessment is essential. PMH: BPD; comorbid depression; prior NSSI; no medical instability. SOCIAL: recent relationship breakup (a classic abandonment trigger); strained but present family; history of difficulty with relationships/employment. The nursing focus across this admission: maintain SAFETY, provide a CONSISTENT therapeutic milieu, manage SPLITTING with a unified team approach, set clear compassionate LIMITS, communicate therapeutically, manage staff COUNTERTRANSFERENCE, and link the patient to evidence-based treatment (DBT) — competencies anchored in coordinated MANAGEMENT OF CARE.
labs.r: BPD = instability of relationships/self-image/affect + impulsivity + abandonment fears + idealization/devaluation + self-harm. NSSI (cutting) relieves emotional pain/dissociation — distinct from suicidal intent, but BPD has elevated SUICIDE risk → assess self-harm AND suicide risk separately + ongoing; never dismiss as 'attention-seeking.' RN role: safety + consistent milieu + manage splitting/limits + therapeutic communication + DBT linkage.

labs.n: Splitting + the consistent, unified team approach
labs.v: SPLITTING is the hallmark defense in BPD: the patient unconsciously experiences people and situations as ALL-GOOD or ALL-BAD and cannot hold both together, so she IDEALIZES some staff ('you're the only one who truly understands me; you're the best nurse here') while DEVALUING others ('the night nurse is cruel and incompetent'). This is not usually a conscious manipulation so much as a way of managing intolerable ambivalence and fear — but its EFFECT is to DIVIDE the treatment team: idealized staff may feel special and bend rules or over-identify, devalued staff may become defensive/punitive, and staff may end up disagreeing with each other about the patient or being played against one another. The MOST IMPORTANT and effective nursing response to splitting is a CONSISTENT, UNIFIED TEAM APPROACH: (1) the entire interdisciplinary team follows the SAME, clearly written CARE PLAN with the SAME limits, privileges, and expectations across ALL staff and ALL shifts; (2) the team COMMUNICATES frequently and openly — regular team huddles, thorough handoffs, and shared documentation — to compare notes, stay aligned, and immediately surface and reconcile any differences in approach (so the patient cannot successfully split staff); (3) individual staff do NOT accept the role of the 'special' idealized caregiver and do NOT disparage or undercut colleagues — instead they gently redirect ('all of the nurses here are working together to help you, and we all follow the same plan'); (4) staff avoid being drawn into secrets, exceptions, or alliances against other team members; and (5) the team presents a united, predictable front, which is paradoxically REASSURING to a patient terrified of instability and abandonment. When a patient idealizes one nurse and devalues another, the correct response is NOT to enjoy the idealization or to defend oneself by criticizing the colleague, but to acknowledge the feeling, avoid taking sides, and reinforce the consistent team plan. CONSISTENCY across the team is the antidote to splitting. THIS PATIENT idealized the day nurse and devalued the night nurse; the team recognized splitting, the day nurse declined the 'special' role and redirected to the shared plan, and a team huddle ensured every staff member applied the same limits — defusing the split.
labs.r: Splitting (idealization/devaluation, all-good/all-bad) divides the team. The key response = a CONSISTENT, UNIFIED TEAM APPROACH: one shared written care plan + same limits across all staff/shifts + frequent team communication/huddles to stay aligned + don't accept the 'special' role or disparage colleagues + don't allow secrets/exceptions/alliances. Consistency is the antidote to splitting. RN role: unified team plan + communication + redirect, don't take sides.

labs.v: LIMIT-SETTING is central and is THERAPEUTIC, not punitive — clear, consistent boundaries provide the predictable structure a patient with BPD needs and counter the chaos/instability of the disorder: (1) establish CLEAR, REASONABLE, CONSISTENT limits and expectations (unit rules, privileges, behavioral expectations) and communicate them to the patient and to the WHOLE team in writing; (2) state limits MATTER-OF-FACTLY, calmly, and respectfully ('the unit rule is X; this applies to everyone'), framing them as caring structure rather than rejection or punishment; (3) apply them UNIFORMLY across all staff and shifts and follow through with CONSISTENT, non-punitive consequences; (4) AVOID power struggles, arguments, and PERSONALIZING the patient's behavior (her anger/devaluation is a symptom of the disorder, not a personal attack); (5) do not negotiate/bend established limits under pressure or in response to idealization, anger, or threats (giving in reinforces the behavior and feeds splitting), while remaining warm and non-rejecting; (6) maintain professional BOUNDARIES — no favoritism, no inappropriate self-disclosure, no 'special' exceptions, no rescuing. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION pairs firmness with empathy: VALIDATE the patient's EMOTION without endorsing the maladaptive BEHAVIOR ('I can see you're feeling overwhelmed and scared — and cutting isn't a safe way to cope; let's find another way'); be honest, reliable, and CONSISTENT (reliability itself reduces the fear of abandonment); name and help the patient label feelings; coach the use of coping/DISTRESS-TOLERANCE skills as alternatives to self-harm; and avoid responses that are rejecting, shaming, sarcastic, dismissive, or that reinforce self-harm with excessive attention/drama. When the patient becomes intensely angry as a limit is held, the nurse stays calm, does not retaliate or capitulate, acknowledges the feeling, restates the limit kindly, and allows the patient to de-escalate. THIS PATIENT became angry when a unit limit was held consistently; staff held the limit matter-of-factly and compassionately, validated her feelings without bending the rule, avoided a power struggle, and she de-escalated and used a coping skill instead of self-harming.
labs.r: Limit-setting is therapeutic structure (not punishment): clear, consistent, reasonable limits stated matter-of-factly + applied uniformly across staff/shifts + consistent non-punitive consequences; don't bend limits under pressure, don't personalize/power-struggle, keep professional boundaries (no favoritism). Therapeutic communication: VALIDATE the emotion, not the maladaptive behavior; be consistent/reliable (counters abandonment fear); coach coping skills; avoid rejecting/shaming responses. RN role: firm + compassionate limits + validating communication.

labs.v: SELF-HARM + SAFETY MANAGEMENT: maintain a SAFE MILIEU — set an appropriate OBSERVATION level, remove/limit access to means of self-harm (sharps, etc.), and search/monitor per unit protocol; assess SUICIDE risk and self-harm risk on an ONGOING basis and SEPARATELY (NSSI is not a suicide attempt, but BPD elevates suicide risk and the two can co-occur — take all self-harm seriously and never assume it is 'just for attention'); respond to an episode of self-injury MATTER-OF-FACTLY — provide necessary wound care calmly and without excessive emotional attention, drama, or lecturing (which can inadvertently REINFORCE the behavior), while never being dismissive of the underlying distress or risk; afterward, help the patient identify the TRIGGER and the FEELING that preceded the act and substitute a coping/distress-tolerance skill; and involve the patient in SAFETY PLANNING (recognizing triggers/warning signs, coping strategies, supports, and crisis resources). STAFF SELF-AWARENESS / COUNTERTRANSFERENCE: patients with BPD reliably evoke strong emotional reactions in staff — frustration, anger, feeling manipulated or 'split,' or conversely rescue fantasies, over-involvement, and feeling 'special' (in idealized staff). Unexamined, these reactions cause staff to act them out — becoming punitive/rejecting or bending rules/over-identifying — which harms the patient and fuels splitting. RECOGNIZING and PROCESSING countertransference (clinical supervision, team debriefs, peer support, self-reflection) is essential to maintaining a consistent, non-punitive, non-idealizing therapeutic stance; it is a professional skill, not a weakness. TREATMENT: PSYCHOTHERAPY is the mainstay, and DIALECTICAL BEHAVIOR THERAPY (DBT) is the gold-standard, evidence-based treatment — teaching skills in MINDFULNESS, DISTRESS TOLERANCE, EMOTION REGULATION, and INTERPERSONAL EFFECTIVENESS (other options: mentalization-based, schema, transference-focused therapy); MEDICATIONS are ADJUNCTIVE and target specific symptoms (mood instability, impulsivity, transient psychosis), with NO drug that cures BPD — clinicians AVOID benzodiazepines (disinhibition, dependence, overdose risk) and minimize polypharmacy, and treat common COMORBIDITIES (depression, PTSD, substance use, eating disorders). PROGNOSIS is more hopeful than stigma suggests: with treatment (especially DBT) and time, many patients achieve significant improvement and remission — so the team conveys realistic HOPE. THIS PATIENT was kept safe, engaged DBT-informed skills, and was linked to outpatient DBT + psychiatry.
labs.r: Safety: safe milieu + appropriate observation + remove means; assess self-harm AND suicide risk ongoing/separately (NSSI ≠ suicide but BPD elevates suicide risk; never 'just attention'); respond to self-injury matter-of-factly (calm wound care, no excessive attention/lecture) + coach coping + safety plan. COUNTERTRANSFERENCE: recognize/process strong staff reactions (supervision/debrief) to stay consistent + non-punitive. Treatment: DBT (mindfulness/distress tolerance/emotion regulation/interpersonal effectiveness) is gold standard; meds adjunctive (avoid benzodiazepines); hopeful prognosis. RN role: safety + self-awareness + DBT linkage.

labs.n: Care coordination/continuity + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: MANAGEMENT OF CARE / CARE COORDINATION is the through-line of effective BPD inpatient care: build and maintain a UNIFIED interdisciplinary CARE PLAN; COORDINATE and align the entire team (nursing across all shifts, the psychiatrist/provider, social work, and therapists) so that limits, privileges, and the therapeutic stance are delivered CONSISTENTLY; use structured COMMUNICATION (team huddles, handoffs, shared documentation) to keep everyone aligned and to prevent splitting; and arrange CONTINUITY of care — referral to outpatient DBT and psychiatry, a written SAFETY PLAN, family/support education, medication reconciliation (symptom-targeted, no benzodiazepines), crisis resources, and timely follow-up. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): staff did NOT accept the idealized 'special' role or criticize the devalued colleague (they redirected to the unified plan); did NOT bend or negotiate limits under pressure, anger, or idealization (consistency maintained); did NOT respond to self-injury with excessive attention/drama OR with dismissiveness ('just attention-seeking') — they treated wounds matter-of-factly while assessing risk seriously; did NOT personalize the patient's anger or get into power struggles; did NOT use benzodiazepines or rely on medication as the primary treatment; and did NOT ignore the elevated SUICIDE risk that coexists with NSSI. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: a SAFE, structured milieu with appropriate observation; a CONSISTENT, unified team that communicated well and defused splitting; clear, compassionate limit-setting; therapeutic, validating communication that built trust and countered abandonment fears; staff who recognized and processed COUNTERTRANSFERENCE; engagement in DBT-informed skills with substitution of coping for self-harm; treatment of comorbid depression; a safety plan + outpatient DBT/psychiatry linkage + support education; and realistic HOPE (BPD is treatable, with high rates of improvement/remission over time). EXPECTED outcome (as here): the patient is kept safe, splitting and limit-testing diminish under a consistent team approach, she begins using skills instead of self-harm, and she is discharged with a safety plan and linkage to evidence-based outpatient treatment. NURSING ROLE: coordinate and sustain a unified, consistent team care plan; set clear compassionate limits; keep the patient safe and assess risk continuously; communicate therapeutically (validate emotions, not maladaptive behavior); manage personal and team countertransference; and connect the patient to DBT and follow-up — recognizing that CONSISTENCY, TEAM COORDINATION, and a non-judgmental therapeutic stance are the most powerful interventions.
labs.r: Care coordination is the through-line: unified interdisciplinary care plan + aligned team (all shifts/disciplines) + structured communication to prevent splitting + continuity (DBT/psychiatry referral, safety plan, family education, follow-up). Negatives confirm correct practice (no 'special' role/colleague-bashing, no bending limits, no excessive-attention or dismissive response to NSSI, no benzodiazepines, didn't ignore suicide risk). Protective: safe milieu + consistent team + limits + validating communication + countertransference awareness + DBT + hope. RN role: coordinate consistent team care + safety + therapeutic stance + DBT linkage.

nursesNote: Inpatient psychiatric unit — 24 y/o woman A. Rivera admitted after a NON-SUICIDAL SELF-INJURY episode (superficial forearm cutting) precipitated by a relationship breakup, with intense distress + fear of abandonment; known BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER + comorbid depression. ADMISSION: alert + oriented; superficial lacerations cleaned/dressed MATTER-OF-FACTLY; tearful + emotionally dysregulated; states cutting 'made the pain stop for a minute' (NSSI to relieve emotional pain — distinct from suicidal intent, but BPD elevates SUICIDE risk → assessed self-harm AND suicide risk SEPARATELY + ongoing; denies current intent/plan today; NOT dismissed as 'attention-seeking'); safe milieu + appropriate observation level + means removed/limited. DAY 1 — SPLITTING: idealizes the day nurse ('you're the only one who understands me') + devalues the night nurse ('she's cruel'); requests special privileges/exceptions. Team recognized SPLITTING → CONSISTENT, UNIFIED approach: day nurse declined the 'special' role + did NOT disparage the colleague + redirected to the shared written CARE PLAN ('we all work together and follow the same plan'); team HUDDLE aligned all staff/shifts on the SAME limits + frequent communication/handoffs to prevent being split. DAY 2 — LIMIT-TESTING + anger: when a unit limit was held consistently she became intensely angry ('you don't care'); staff set the limit MATTER-OF-FACTLY + compassionately (NOT punitively), validated her FEELINGS without bending the rule, avoided a power struggle, did NOT personalize her anger, and remained consistent → she de-escalated + used a coping skill instead of self-harming. DAY 4 — SKILLS: engaging in DBT-informed groups (mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness); substituting coping skills for self-harm; consistent team approach reduced splitting/limit-testing; affect more regulated; ongoing risk assessment. DISCHARGE PLANNING: stabilized; written SAFETY PLAN (triggers, coping skills, supports, crisis contacts); linked to outpatient DBT + psychiatry follow-up; comorbid depression addressed; medications adjunctive/symptom-targeted (NO benzodiazepines, minimal polypharmacy) reconciled; family/support education; hopeful, realistic messaging that BPD is treatable (high improvement/remission with DBT over time). TEAM REFLECTION: debriefed COUNTERTRANSFERENCE — some staff felt 'special'/protective, others angry/manipulated; recognizing + processing these reactions (supervision/team support) enabled a consistent, non-punitive, non-idealizing stance + prevented splitting from dividing the team. CARE COORDINATION (management of care) was the through-line: unified interdisciplinary care plan + aligned team across shifts/disciplines + structured communication + continuity (DBT/psychiatry/safety plan/follow-up).
providerOrders: ADMIT to inpatient psychiatric unit; borderline personality disorder with non-suicidal self-injury; comorbid depression
DIAGNOSIS: borderline personality disorder; NSSI (forearm cutting) precipitated by interpersonal stressor; ongoing suicide-risk assessment
CODE STATUS: full code; alert with capacity
SAFETY:
Maintain a safe milieu; appropriate observation level; remove/limit access to means of self-harm (sharps, etc.) per protocol
Assess suicide risk AND self-harm risk on an ongoing basis and separately (NSSI is not a suicide attempt, but BPD carries elevated suicide risk; never dismiss self-harm as 'attention-seeking')
Respond to self-injury matter-of-factly (calm wound care, no excessive attention/drama or lecturing; never dismissive of risk); develop a written safety plan with the patient
MANAGE SPLITTING — consistent, unified team approach:
One clearly written interdisciplinary care plan with the SAME limits/privileges/expectations applied across ALL staff and ALL shifts
Frequent team communication (huddles, thorough handoffs, shared documentation) to stay aligned and surface/reconcile any differences
Do NOT accept the 'special'/idealized role or disparage devalued colleagues; redirect to the unified plan; do not permit secrets/exceptions/staff alliances
LIMIT-SETTING + THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION:
Set clear, reasonable, consistent limits; state them matter-of-factly + compassionately (caring structure, not punishment); apply uniformly with consistent non-punitive consequences

Do not bend/negotiate limits under pressure, anger, idealization, or threats; avoid power struggles + do not personalize the patient's behavior; maintain professional boundaries (no favoritism/self-disclosure/exceptions)

Validate the patient's emotions WITHOUT endorsing maladaptive behavior; be honest/reliable/consistent (counters abandonment fear); coach coping/distress-tolerance skills as alternatives to self-harm; avoid rejecting/shaming responses
STAFF SELF-AWARENESS:

Recognize and process countertransference (frustration/feeling manipulated, or rescue/over-identification) via clinical supervision, team debriefs, and peer support to maintain a consistent, non-punitive, non-idealizing stance
TREATMENT + CARE COORDINATION:
Psychotherapy is the mainstay; refer to/engage Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) — mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

Medications adjunctive + symptom-targeted (mood instability/impulsivity); AVOID benzodiazepines (disinhibition/dependence/overdose) and minimize polypharmacy; treat comorbidities (depression, PTSD, substance use, eating disorders)

Coordinate the interdisciplinary team (psychiatry, nursing, social work, therapy); ensure continuity — outpatient DBT + psychiatry follow-up, family/support education, crisis resources, timely follow-up
EDUCATION + HOPE:
Educate the patient/family that BPD is treatable, with high rates of improvement/remission over time (especially with DBT); reinforce coping skills + the safety plan
Document objectively: risk assessments, limits, the consistent care plan, the patient's responses, and the treatment/follow-up plan

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this inpatient BPD presentation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Borderline personality disorder features — abandonment fears, unstable intense relationships (idealization/devaluation), affective instability, impulsivity, and recurrent self-harm — precipitated by a relationship breakup
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing the BPD pattern frames the whole therapeutic approach. RN role: recognize features + plan accordingly.

opts.text: Non-suicidal self-injury (cutting) often relieves emotional pain/dissociation and is distinct from suicidal intent — BUT BPD carries elevated suicide risk, so assess self-harm AND suicide risk separately and continuously
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — NSSI vs suicide distinction + ongoing risk assessment; never dismiss as 'attention-seeking.' RN role: separate, ongoing risk assessment.

opts.text: Splitting (idealizing one nurse, devaluing another) is emerging and can divide the team — requiring a consistent, unified team approach
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — splitting is the hallmark defense; consistency is the antidote. RN role: recognize splitting + unified team response.

opts.text: Clear, consistent, compassionate limit-setting (applied uniformly across staff/shifts) provides therapeutic structure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — limit-setting is central + therapeutic. RN role: consistent, non-punitive limits.

opts.text: A safe milieu with appropriate observation and removal/limiting of means of self-harm
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — milieu safety is foundational. RN role: safe environment + observation.

opts.text: Staff countertransference (feeling 'special,' or angry/manipulated) must be recognized and processed to stay consistent and non-punitive
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — unexamined countertransference fuels splitting/punitive or rule-bending responses. RN role: self-awareness + team support.

opts.text: Linkage to evidence-based treatment — DBT — and care coordination/continuity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — DBT is the gold standard; coordinated continuity matters. RN role: DBT referral + care coordination.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Since she says you are the only nurse who truly understands her, accept that special role and grant her a few privileges the other nurses won't, to build rapport
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Accepting the idealized 'special' role and bending rules feeds splitting, undermines the team, and is not therapeutic. Redirect to the unified plan and apply consistent limits across all staff.

opts.text: Treat the self-injury as attention-seeking and ignore it so as not to reinforce the behavior
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Dismissing self-harm as 'attention-seeking' and ignoring it is stigmatizing and unsafe (BPD has elevated suicide risk). Provide calm matter-of-fact wound care, assess risk seriously, and address the underlying distress — without excessive drama.

opts.text: Splitting (all-good/all-bad; idealization/devaluation) is the hallmark BPD defense, and a consistent, unified team approach with frequent communication is the most effective response
opts.rat: CORRECT — splitting + the consistency antidote. Foundation for team management.

opts.text: Limit-setting is therapeutic structure (not punishment): clear, consistent limits applied uniformly with consistent consequences; bending limits under pressure reinforces maladaptive behavior
opts.rat: CORRECT — limit-setting principle. Foundation for a therapeutic milieu.

opts.text: Therapeutic communication validates the patient's emotion without endorsing the maladaptive behavior, and reliability/consistency counters the fear of abandonment
opts.rat: CORRECT — validating-but-boundaried communication. Foundation for trust-building.

opts.text: NSSI is distinct from a suicide attempt (it often relieves emotional pain), but BPD carries elevated suicide risk, so both are assessed separately and continuously
opts.rat: CORRECT — NSSI vs suicide + risk principle. Foundation for safe assessment.

opts.text: Staff predictably experience strong countertransference reactions, which must be recognized and processed (supervision/team support) to remain consistent and non-punitive
opts.rat: CORRECT — countertransference principle. Foundation for maintaining the therapeutic stance.

opts.text: DBT is the gold-standard psychotherapy; medications are adjunctive and symptom-targeted, benzodiazepines are avoided, and prognosis is hopeful with treatment over time
opts.rat: CORRECT — treatment principle. Foundation for evidence-based care + realistic hope.

opts.text: The best way to handle splitting is for the idealized nurse to provide most of the care while the devalued nurse stays away, to keep the patient calm
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Reinforcing the idealized/devalued split (and avoidance) entrenches splitting and divides the team. All staff apply the same consistent approach + communicate; no one accepts the 'special' role.

opts.text: Firm limits should be presented as punishment so the patient learns consequences for manipulative behavior
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Limits are therapeutic structure delivered matter-of-factly and compassionately, NOT punishment; punitive framing damages the therapeutic relationship and is countertherapeutic.

opts.text: Safety — maintain a safe milieu/observation, remove means of self-harm, and assess suicide and self-harm risk continuously
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Physical safety + risk assessment come first (elevated suicide risk in BPD). RN role: safety + risk assessment.

opts.text: Consistent, unified team approach to manage splitting — one shared care plan, same limits across staff/shifts, frequent team communication
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Consistency is the antidote to splitting and underpins all care. RN role: unified team plan + communication.

opts.text: Clear, compassionate limit-setting + therapeutic communication (validate emotions, not maladaptive behavior)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Therapeutic structure + validating communication build trust and reduce maladaptive behavior. RN role: limits + therapeutic communication.

opts.text: Staff self-awareness/countertransference management to sustain a consistent, non-punitive stance
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Managing staff reactions prevents splitting and punitive/rule-bending responses. RN role: self-awareness + team support.

opts.text: Treatment linkage + care coordination — DBT referral, safety plan, comorbidity treatment, family education, and follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Important for recovery/continuity. RN role: DBT linkage + coordinated continuity.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED in this BPD care.

opts.text: Maintain a safe milieu with appropriate observation, limit access to means of self-harm, and assess suicide/self-harm risk continuously
opts.rat: Core safety management. RN role: safe environment + risk assessment.

opts.text: Use a consistent, unified team care plan with the same limits across all staff/shifts and frequent team communication to prevent splitting
opts.rat: The key response to splitting. RN role: unified team approach.

opts.text: Set clear limits matter-of-factly and compassionately, applying them uniformly without bending under pressure or personalizing the patient's anger
opts.rat: Therapeutic limit-setting. RN role: consistent, non-punitive limits.

opts.text: Validate the patient's emotions while not endorsing maladaptive behavior, and coach distress-tolerance/coping skills instead of self-harm
opts.rat: Therapeutic communication + skills coaching. RN role: validate + coach skills.

opts.text: Provide calm, matter-of-fact wound care for self-injury without excessive attention, while taking the underlying distress and risk seriously
opts.rat: Appropriate, non-reinforcing, non-dismissive response to NSSI. RN role: matter-of-fact care + risk assessment.

opts.text: Recognize and process staff countertransference (supervision/debrief) and refer to DBT with coordinated follow-up + a safety plan
opts.rat: Self-awareness + evidence-based treatment + continuity. RN role: countertransference management + DBT linkage.

opts.text: Accept the idealized 'special' role, grant her privileges other nurses won't, and agree that the night nurse is a bad nurse
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — accepting idealization, bending rules, and disparaging colleagues feed splitting and divide the team. Redirect to the unified plan; apply consistent limits.

opts.text: Ignore the self-injury entirely as attention-seeking so as not to reinforce it
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — dismissing/ignoring self-harm is stigmatizing and unsafe (elevated suicide risk). Provide calm care + serious risk assessment.

opts.text: Present limits and consequences as punishment for manipulative behavior
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — limits are therapeutic structure delivered matter-of-factly, not punishment; punitive framing is countertherapeutic.

opts.text: Give a standing benzodiazepine as the primary long-term treatment for her BPD
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — psychotherapy (DBT) is the mainstay; medications are adjunctive/symptom-targeted, and benzodiazepines are avoided (disinhibition/dependence/overdose).

opts.text: Negotiate and relax the established unit limits whenever she becomes angry or threatens to feel abandoned, to keep her calm
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — bending limits under pressure reinforces maladaptive behavior and feeds splitting. Hold limits consistently and compassionately while validating feelings.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Ensure safety — safe milieu/observation, remove means of self-harm, and assess suicide/self-harm risk
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety + risk assessment precede everything. RN role: safety.

opts.text: Establish/follow a consistent, unified team care plan and communicate with the team (huddle) to align on limits and prevent splitting
opts.rat: #2 — Second. The unified plan + team alignment underpin all subsequent care. RN role: unified plan + team communication.

opts.text: Set clear, compassionate limits and use therapeutic communication (validate emotions, coach coping skills) consistently
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Deliver the consistent limits + validating communication day to day. RN role: limits + therapeutic communication.

opts.text: Recognize/process staff countertransference (debrief/supervision) to sustain the consistent, non-punitive stance
opts.rat: #4 — Ongoing. Managing staff reactions keeps the approach consistent. RN role: self-awareness + team support.

opts.text: Coordinate treatment + continuity — DBT referral, safety plan, comorbidity treatment, family education, and follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — For recovery/continuity. RN role: DBT linkage + coordinated follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action taken, classify as Appropriate or Inappropriate.

opts.text: When idealized as the 'only nurse who understands,' the nurse declined the special role, did not disparage the colleague, and redirected to the shared team plan; a huddle aligned all staff on the same limits
opts.rat: Correct response to splitting — consistency + team alignment. Model handling.

opts.text: When a limit was held and the patient became angry, staff stayed calm, validated her feelings, restated the limit matter-of-factly without bending it, and avoided a power struggle
opts.rat: Therapeutic, consistent limit-setting + validation. Model handling.

opts.text: Self-injury was treated with calm, matter-of-fact wound care while suicide/self-harm risk was assessed seriously and coping skills were coached
opts.rat: Non-reinforcing, non-dismissive, safety-focused response to NSSI. Model handling.

opts.text: The team debriefed their countertransference (some felt special/protective, others angry) and used supervision to maintain a consistent, non-punitive stance
opts.rat: Healthy management of countertransference. Model handling.

opts.text: The idealized nurse accepted the special role, granted extra privileges the other nurses wouldn't, and agreed the night nurse was incompetent
opts.rat: ⚠️ Feeds splitting + divides the team + bends limits. Incorrect — redirect to the unified plan and apply consistent limits.

opts.text: Staff ignored the self-injury entirely as 'just attention-seeking' and did not assess risk
opts.rat: ⚠️ Stigmatizing + unsafe (elevated suicide risk). Incorrect — provide calm care + serious risk assessment.

opts.text: Limits were relaxed each time the patient became angry or threatened to feel abandoned, to keep her calm
opts.rat: ⚠️ Bending limits under pressure reinforces maladaptive behavior and feeds splitting. Incorrect — hold limits consistently and compassionately.

opts.text: The patient was referred to outpatient DBT with a written safety plan, comorbidity treatment, and follow-up, with adjunctive (non-benzodiazepine) medication only
opts.rat: Evidence-based treatment linkage + continuity + appropriate medication approach. Model handling.

opts.text: Limits were framed and delivered as punishment for manipulative behavior
opts.rat: ⚠️ Punitive framing is countertherapeutic; limits are caring, matter-of-fact structure. Incorrect.

opts.text: By discharge, with the consistent team approach, splitting/limit-testing had decreased and the patient was using coping skills instead of self-harm
opts.rat: Expected positive response to consistent, coordinated, therapeutic care. Model outcome.
